author: jordancoinbase_10730
content:
> proposers are incentivized to publicize bids to force builders into an iterative competition, which maximizes the proposer's own revenue as relays do today

I question this assumption.

Open bid auctions are beneficial generally when blocks have similar value to all participating bidders, but builders have private order flow.

My gut is that this is generally true, usually blocks have similar value to most builders, but it puts a high value to latency games.

The flip side would be times of high information assymetry, such as jackpots, where a builder has private order flow that is highly profitable, they can see others don't have that and optimize their return. With a sealed bid system you don't have this information, and bids must be delivered more based on the value you have and think others may have.

The question is how similar are the blocks in value to each bidder. If only exceptionally high bids are not similar across bidders, it could be interesting to do a hybrid where bids up to X are open, and bids above are sealed, Where the only information available to a bidder would be that sealed prices have been received.

author: jvranek
content:
> The question is how similar are the blocks in value to each bidder.

something potentially interesting https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.16333

> Open bid auctions are beneficial generally when blocks have similar value to all participating bidders, but builders have private order flow.

A question is who are we optimizing for? Builders [raised the point](https://github.com/ethereum/builder-specs/issues/156) that open bidding helps lower the barrier to entry in the presence of exclusive flow, since having full info about other builders' bids allows new builders to focus on technology + capital rather than requiring sophisticated models to try to predict how to outbid the other incumbent builders who have the structural advantage (flow). This also would help proposer revenue since the builders are iteratively outbidding each other. 

I think it's true though that this only holds when there is sufficient competition. If there's a dominant builder, then paying the second price is likely much lower than their true valuation. But same logic applies to when their's sealed bids, the dominant builder will shade less than their true value but still beat the second best builder (we can assume the dominant builder can afford quants to model how to outbid second place while in a monopoly). 

Maybe a way to think of it is:
1. when there's open bidding it's easier to enter the builder market. But if there's a dominant builder it doesn't really matter since they can always win
2. when there's sealed bidding it's harder to enter the builder market. If there's a dominant builder it's slightly harder for them to figure out how much to shade to win with margin but they still will

since ePBS doesn't solve the structural market issues around exclusive flow, I think it's more pragmatic to solve for 1 and try to keep the barrier to entry low, rather than expedite 2
